question_title: How many total goals will be scored across the two 2026 World Cup quarterfinals on July 9 and 10?
summary: Self-contained statistical question: total goals in two 2026 World Cup quarterfinals (July 9 & 10), counting regulation + extra time, excluding shootouts. Teams are unknown, so this is a pure scoring-distribution model. Monte Carlo using Gamma-Poisson (negative binomial) per-match goals — capturing overdispersion — plus an extra-time component (~27-30% of knockout matches go to ET, low ET goal mean), mixed across a higher (reg mean 2.35) and lower (reg mean 2.10) scenario. Result: combined mean ~4.75, median ~4-5, right-skewed with a tail to 8+, P0≈2%, P≥10≈6%. This matches the question's stated typical range (4-5) with plausible 0-2 lows and 8+ highs. Centered on the model mean rather than a downshifted defensive estimate, since 2026 group-stage scoring is running above recent norms.

factors.description: WC knockout matches average ~2.3-2.5 goals/match including extra time; two matches imply combined mean ~4.5-5.0
Goal counts are overdispersed Poisson; right-skewed distribution with meaningful tail toward 8+ if either match is high-scoring
Knockout games often defensive raising low-total mass (2-4), but 2026 group stage running high-scoring offsets downward pull; ~27% go to ET adding modest goals

reasoning:
Forecast submitted.

**Summary:** This is a self-contained statistical question about total goals in two 2026 World Cup quarterfinals (regulation + extra time, excluding shootouts). Since the teams are unknown at forecast time, I modeled it as a pure scoring-distribution problem using a Monte Carlo simulation:

- **Per-match goals**: Gamma-Poisson (negative binomial) to capture overdispersion, base knockout rate ~2.2-2.35 goals/match
- **Extra time**: ~27-30% of knockout matches go to ET, with a low ET goal mean
- **Scenario mixture**: 50/50 blend of higher-scoring (attacking) and lower-scoring (defensive) quarterfinal scenarios

**Result:** Combined mean ~4.75, median ~4-5, right-skewed.

**Distribution (percentiles):**
| P10 | P20 | P40 | P50 | P60 | P80 | P90 |
|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 1.8 | 2.5 | 3.9 | 4.5 | 5.1 | 6.8 | 8.1 |

The premortem reviewer flagged that I had initially pulled the center down to 4.3 on a defensive-football argument that 2026's actual (high-scoring) group-stage data doesn't support, so I recentered on the model mean (~4.75). The distribution matches the question's stated typical range of 4-5 with appropriate tails for low (0-2) and high (8+) outcomes.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as the sum of goals scored in the two FIFA World Cup 2026 quarterfinal matches played on July 9, 2026 (Gillette Stadium, Foxborough) and July 10, 2026 (SoFi Stadium, Inglewood), counting all goals scored during regulation time (90 minutes) plus extra time (if played), as reported in the official final scores on FIFA's fixtures and results page: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/scores-fixtures . For example, if the July 9 match finishes 2-1 and the July 10 match finishes 1-1 (then decided on penalties), the combined total is 2+1+1+1 = 5, and the question resolves 5.
fine_print: Only goals counted toward the official match score (regulation plus any extra time) are included; goals scored in a penalty shootout are NOT counted, as FIFA records shootout results separately from the match score. An own goal counts as a goal for the team credited with the score and is included in the total. The two relevant matches are the quarterfinals officially scheduled for July 9, 2026 and July 10, 2026; if either match is postponed, rescheduled, abandoned, or awarded by walkover/forfeit, the official final score (or 0 goals for an abandoned/forfeited match with no recorded goals) as published by FIFA for that fixture will be used. If the FIFA fixtures page is unavailable, the final scores as reported by other credible sources (https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) will be used.

factors.description: WC knockout matches historically average ~2.3-2.5 goals/match including extra time; two matches imply a combined mean near 4.5-5.0
Goal counts are overdispersed Poisson; right-skewed with a meaningful tail toward 8+ if either match is high-scoring
Knockout games are often tight/defensive, raising mass on low totals (2-4); ~25-30% go to ET adding modest goals

input.anchor: Knockout-stage goals/match ~2.4 (FIFA WC historical), two matches => combined mean ~4.75
input.assessment: Self-contained statistical question on goals in two WC quarterfinals. Teams unknown, so this is a pure scoring-rate model. Monte Carlo with negative-binomial (Gamma-Poisson) per-match goals plus extra-time component, mixed across a higher (reg mean 2.35) and lower (reg mean 2.10) scenario gives combined mean ~4.75, median ~4-5, right-skewed. P0=2.3%, modal 3-4, P>=10 ~5.7%. Distribution well-matches question's stated 4-5 typical with 0-2 low and 8+ high tails.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether both matches are defensive (pushes toward 2-4) or one becomes a blowout (pushes toward 8+). Extra-time frequency adds modest upside.
input.tool_audit: execute_code used for Monte Carlo simulation — worked cleanly. No external research needed since teams are unknown and this is a scoring-distribution problem; no tools failed.
input.process_reflection: Smooth for this question type — a single simulation suffices. The discrete numeric scaffolding worked well. No friction; would not have benefited from research subagents since matchups are inherently unknown at forecast time.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
Methodology is sound and the core evidence chain is clean. The historical anchor (~2.4 goals/match for WC knockout matches) checks out against web evidence (overall WC ~2.48-2.69/match, knockout slightly lower). The Monte Carlo construction (Gamma-Poisson overdispersion + ~27-30% ET probability with a low ET goal mean) is appropriate, and all three factors map honestly to the simulation outputs (combined mean ~4.75, median 4-5, right-skewed tail to 8+). No hallucinated evidence, no double-counting, no wrong-direction factors. Resolution criteria are fully provided and the model engages with them correctly (regulation + ET, excluding shootouts, summing both matches). Tool audit is accurate vs ground-truth metrics (0 errors; web_search/stock_price were non-load-bearing).

Two concerns, neither forecast-breaking:

1) UNUSED FRESH WORLDVIEW RESEARCH ON THE AGENT'S OWN DECISIVE DIMENSION. The agent's "what would change my mind" explicitly states that "Round-of-16 scoring data from this specific tournament showing whether 2026 is running high-scoring or defensive would move me materially." The worldview store contains directly relevant data: notes/worldview/archive/2026-fifa-world-cup-goals-per-match-statistics-gro-dacec255.json shows 2026 running ~3.05 goals/match through 20 Matchday 1 games — significantly ABOVE recent norms (~2.65). The agent never consulted this. Mitigating factors: (a) it is group-stage data, which does not translate cleanly to the systematically lower-scoring, more defensive knockout rounds the question concerns; (b) the entry is past its stale_after date (2026-06-17) by the forecast date (2026-06-29); (c) the 3.05 figure is inflated by early blowouts (Germany 7-1, Sweden 5-1). So it is suggestive context rather than a clean contradiction — but the agent should have checked its own research on the exact factor it flagged as material.

2) CENTER PULLED BELOW BOTH MODEL MEAN AND ANCHOR, IN THE WRONG DIRECTION RELATIVE TO TOURNAMENT CONTEXT. The simulation mean is 4.75, the anchor is 4.75, and the factors center at 4.0-5.0, yet the tentative center is 4.3. The downward shift is justified by a defensive-football counterargument (2018/2022 cagey knockouts) that this tournament's actual scoring data (running high) does not support. The shift is modest and within Monte Carlo noise, so it does not break the forecast — but combining an unsupported downshift with the ignored high-scoring evidence means the center plausibly belongs closer to the model's ~4.6-4.8 than 4.3. My independent estimate: center ~4.6-4.8 with the agent's submitted range (2-8) being well-calibrated. The gap from 4.3 is small enough to be a warn rather than a fail.

The counterargument and confidence (0.75) are reasonable and appropriately calibrated for a teams-unknown statistical problem.
